ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific 'lamb tagine' entry; closest match is lamb stew (FOODON_03311737). Tagine is a specific North African slow-cooked preparation distinct from generic stew.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific tagine dish code. A0CKW (lamb meat, cooked) is the closest defensible anchor. The dish also contains vegetables, dried fruit, and spices not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for lamb tagine as a prepared dish.

serving.description: 1 plate with lamb stew and side of couscous or rice
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of lamb tagine is approximately 500 g, comprising roughly 300 g of the stew itself (lamb, vegetables, dried fruit, sauce) and 200 g of couscous or rice as accompaniment.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Lamb, domestic, composite of trimmed retail cuts, cooked (NDB 17003) and component vegetables/legumes
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for lamb tagine as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked lamb (NDB 17003), cooked chickpeas (NDB 16057), cooked carrots (NDB 11124), canned tomatoes (NDB 11529), dried apricots (NDB 09021), and olive oil (NDB 04053), weighted by typical recipe proportions. Input record values (energy 150 kcal, protein 11 g, fat 7 g, carbohydrate 10 g, sugar 3 g, sodium 170 mg, dietary fibre 2 g, calcium 16 mg, iron 1.2 mg, cholesterol 36 mg, saturated fat 2.4 g, trans fat 0.1 g, vitamin C 5 mg per 100 g) are consistent with USDA component-based estimates and are retained with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Atkinson et al. 2008; University of Sydney GI Database — chickpea and lamb stew analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lamb tagine. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributors: chickpeas (GI ~28, University of Sydney GI Database) and dried apricots (GI ~31, Atkinson et al. 2008). Carrots and tomatoes contribute minimal glycaemic load. The high protein (11 g/100 g) and fat (7 g/100 g) content further attenuate glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per 500 g serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (35 × 50 g) / 100 = 17.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (170 mg/100 g) with estimated high potassium (~320 mg/100 g) from lamb, chickpeas, tomatoes, carrots, and dried fruit, yielding a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio.
hypertension.guidance: Lamb tagine is suitable for hypertensive individuals when prepared with low-sodium or homemade broth; patients should avoid adding table salt and monitor sodium from accompaniments such as couscous prepared with salted water.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (10 g/100 g) with low estimated GI (~35) driven by chickpeas; high protein (11 g/100 g) and dietary fibre (2 g/100 g) slow gastric emptying and moderate postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume lamb tagine in moderate portions (approximately 300 g stew without accompaniment), substituting couscous with cauliflower couscous or reducing rice portion to limit total carbohydrate load; dried fruit quantity in the recipe should be minimised to reduce concentrated natural sugar intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (2.4 g/100 g) from lamb and cholesterol (36 mg/100 g) are moderate; olive oil provides monounsaturated fat and anti-inflammatory oleocanthal; anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, cinnamon) may confer additional vascular benefit.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit lamb tagine to 1–2 servings per week, choose lean lamb cuts, use olive oil as the cooking fat, and pair with non-refined carbohydrate accompaniments to manage overall saturated fat and glycaemic load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lamb is red meat containing arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6) relevant to RA pathogenesis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Lamb tagine is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid load of lamb; substituting lamb with chicken breast or chickpeas and retaining the anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric, ginger, cinnamon, saffron) and olive oil would make the dish RA-suitable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb tagine is a traditional North African slow-cooked stew originating from Morocco, named after the conical earthenware pot (tagine or tajine) in which it is traditionally prepared. The dish features tender braised lamb — typically shoulder or leg cuts — combined with chickpeas, root vegetables such as carrots and turnips, tomatoes, onions, and dried fruits including apricots and prunes, all simmered in a richly spiced broth. The characteristic spice blend includes turmeric, cumin, coriander, cinnamon, ginger, saffron, and garlic, which impart a warm, aromatic, and mildly sweet-savoury flavour profile. The slow-cooking process — typically 1.5 to 2.5 hours at low heat — renders the lamb exceptionally tender and allows the sauce to reduce into a thick, flavourful gravy. Lamb tagine is typically served with couscous, flatbread, or steamed rice. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 150 kcal, 11 g protein, 7 g fat (2.4 g saturated), 10 g carbohydrate, 2 g dietary fibre, and 170 mg sodium. The low glycaemic index (~35) driven by chickpeas and the high protein content make it a moderate-glycaemic dish suitable for most adults. The dish is naturally halal and gluten-free when served without bread. Clinically, the moderate saturated fat from lamb warrants portion awareness for cardiovascular patients, while the low sodium and high potassium profile support blood pressure management. The anti-inflammatory spice profile (curcumin from turmeric, gingerols from ginger) provides additional functional food value.
common_user_queries: Is lamb tagine suitable for diabetics?
How many calories in lamb tagine?
What spices are in lamb tagine?
Can I eat lamb tagine with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No single approved source (USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD) contains a direct entry for lamb tagine as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries for cooked lamb, chickpeas, carrots, tomatoes, dried apricots, and olive oil, weighted by typical recipe proportions. Input record values are consistent with these estimates and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on chickpea and dried apricot GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-based estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central (cooked lamb NDB 17003, chickpeas NDB 16057, carrots NDB 11124, canned tomatoes NDB 11529, dried apricots NDB 09021, olive oil NDB 04053). No direct approved-source entry for lamb tagine as a prepared dish exists. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.8) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.6) are estimated from USDA lamb and olive oil fatty acid profiles; potassium_mg (320) is estimated from component contributions. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient breakdown for this composite dish preparation is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024; individual component values exist but reliable weighted composite figures cannot be confirmed without recipe-level analysis. 4. Input cultural metadata incorrectly listed primary_cuisine as 'Turkish' and ethnic_tags as 'Turkish, Kebab, Shawarma'; corrected to Moroccan/North African. Country of origin corrected from 'Singapore' to 'Morocco'. 5. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for lamb tagine found in University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. 6. The dish is not a Singaporean dish; G6 multilingual requirements for Singaporean dishes (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply. No mandatory local-language names are required for Moroccan cuisine under the scoring model.
